retrieved_docs.title: Pantothenate Kinase-Associated Neurodegeneration (PKAN) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A mutation in the PANK2 gene accounts for the majority of inherited PKAN cases, serving as the etiology of PKAN in various studies. Previous studies mentioned that PANK2 mutations are associated with all cases of classic Hallervorden-Spatz syndrome and one-third of cases of atypical disease. At the same time, predicted levels of pantothenate kinase 2 protein were correlated with the severity of the disease. [14]

This knowledge is essential for exploring potential therapeutic options, including gene therapy approaches that could offer a more definitive future solution.

retrieved_docs.title: Steppage Gait -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Any damage or compression of the neuraxis anywhere along this pathway can potentially lead to foot drop and steppage gait. Frequent reasons include traumatic injuries, pelvic fractures, tibia or fibular head fractures, tight casts, prolonged positioning in lithotomy during surgical procedures, compression by a space-occupying lesion, vascular impairments as in conditions as lupus, or Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia which can lead to vasculitis causing nerve ischemia. Neuropathies affecting the nerves of the peripheral nervous system are referred to as peripheral neuropathies. These can be primarily demyelinating, primarily axonal, or mixed. Primary demyelinating neuropathies can be acquired or congenital. Examples of acquired neuropathies include acquired inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP) and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP). Hereditary neuropathies include Charcot-Marie Tooth (CMT), and hereditary preponderance to pressure palsies (HNPP). GNE (UDP-N-acetylglucosamine 2-epimerase/N-acetylmannosamine kinase) myopathy (Nonaka myopathy), GARS1 (Glycyl-TRNA Synthetase 1)-associated axonal neuropathy, and alkaptonuria are rare causes. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are now at least 14 different known MODY mutations. They include GCK, HNF1A, HNF4A, HNF1B, INS,  NEURO1, PDX1, PAX4, ABCC8, KCNJ11, KLF11, CEL, BLK, and APPL1 . The different genes vary with respect to the age of onset, response to treatment, and the presence of extra-pancreatic manifestations. The most common gene mutations are the following: Gene mutation in the hepatocyte nuclear factor 1 alpha ( HNF1A ) accounts for 30% to 60% of MODY. Gene mutation in the hepatocyte nuclear factor 4 alpha ( HNF4A ) accounts for 5% to 10% of MODY cases. Gene mutations in glucokinase ( GCK ) account for 30% to 60% of the cases of MODY. Gene mutation in hepatocyte nuclear factor 1 beta ( HNF1B ) accounts for less than 5% of the cases of MODY. The difference in the prevalence of the MODY genes varies from country to country, which may be, in part, due to differences in reporting.

retrieved_docs.title: Westphal Sign -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: An absent or diminished patellar tendon reflex may be due to PNS pathology affecting either the afferent sensory neurons or the efferent motor neurons. If the reflex is absent or diminished combined with sensory loss, the lesion is likely in the afferent sensory nerves. [9] Examples of diseases that fit this category include sensory polyneuropathies, spinocerebellar degenerations, and root avulsions. [1] If the reflex is absent or diminished in combination with paralysis, muscle atrophy, or fasciculations, the pathology would more likely involve the efferent motor component of the reflex. Examples of diseases that fit this category include motor neuronopathy, radiculopathy, and neuromuscular junction disorders, such as Lambert Eaton syndrome. [1] Many additional causes of peripheral neuropathy can yield an absent or diminished patellar tendon reflex, including diabetes, alcohol use disorder, amyloidosis, vitamin deficiencies, toxins, and remote cancer. [9] Thus the observation of Westphal’s sign carries little specificity but can support the diagnosis of pathology of the PNS.

retrieved_docs.title: Foot Drop -- Etiology -- Neurological Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease: Charcot-Marie-Tooth (CMT) is a primary congenital demyelinating peripheral neuropathy and one of the most common inherited neuropathies, affecting approximately 1 in 25,000 individuals. This condition affects both motor and sensory nerves, causing foot drop and wasting of the lower leg muscles, resulting in a characteristic "stork leg" appearance. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurotrophic Keratitis -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Severe cases may present with corneal ulceration or stromal thinning without preceding pain. Nonhealing PEDs or secondary infections may result from epithelial compromise. Neurotrophic keratitis is often secondary to systemic or ocular conditions affecting the trigeminal nerve. The following must be carefully assessed: History of herpetic eye disease (HSV, HZO) Diabetes mellitus (peripheral neuropathy affecting corneal nerves) Ocular surgeries (LASIK, PRK, corneal transplantation, trigeminal nerve decompression) Neurological disorders (stroke, brainstem tumors, multiple sclerosis) Chemical or thermal burns (exposure to acids, alkalis, and temperature extremes) Chronic topical medication use (glaucoma drugs, anesthetic abuse, preservatives like BAK) Autoimmune diseases (Sjögren syndrome, sarcoidosis) Congenital conditions (Riley-Day syndrome, Moebius syndrome, familial dysautonomia)

retrieved_docs.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Peripheral Neuropathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Common symptoms of most peripheral polyneuropathies involve paresthesias, numbness, or pain in the distal extremities, such as in the feet or hands. Some can present acutely, while many have a more insidious onset. A patient may describe difficulty with buttoning their clothes or tripping over their feet, which may be signs of worsening weakness and progression of the disease. Inherited polyneuropathies may present with ataxia and muscle cramping, while acquired polyneuropathies typically present as burning or paraesthesias. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2391
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerves (periferní nervy) serve as the primary anatomic site for several inherited and structural neuropathic disorders, notably Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو- ماري- توث) and the horseshoe‑tail syndrome associated with neurogenic bladder (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية). In addition, peripheral nerves are the anatomical location where a wide spectrum of neuropathic findings can be identified. Drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik) manifests within the peripheral nerves, as do metabolic and systemic neuropathies such as diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري). Multiple peripheral nerve involvement is also observed in malignant diseases (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث) and in collagen‑vascular disorders (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني). Inflammatory conditions can affect the peripheral nerves as well, exemplified by acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) when it extends to neural tissue, and by mononeuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي), which involves isolated peripheral nerve inflammation. Collectively, these relationships underscore the central role of peripheral nerves as both the primary site of certain hereditary neuropathies and the common locus for diverse acquired neuropathic, inflammatory, and systemic disorders, highlighting the importance of thorough peripheral nerve assessment in comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2488
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerves (periferní nervy) serve as the primary anatomic site for Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث) and are the principal structures involved in a wide spectrum of neuropathic disorders. Drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik) manifests with findings in the peripheral nerves, as do diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), malignant‑disease‑associated multifocal neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), and severe neuropathy of high clinical significance (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة). These interconnections underscore the central role of peripheral nerves in the pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of diverse neuropathies. The inclusion of acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) as a finding site for peripheral nerves is inconsistent with the other relationships and likely represents a misclassification, as bronchial infection does not directly involve peripheral nerve pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2651
retrieved_docs.content: Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ ماري‑ توث) primarily involves the peripheral nerves (periferní nervy), as do several other neuropathic conditions. Drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik) also manifests in the peripheral nerves, highlighting the neurotoxic potential of certain medications. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين) is another disorder whose pathological findings are localized to the peripheral nerves, reflecting an immune‑mediated demyelination process. Diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري) similarly targets peripheral nerves, underscoring the metabolic complications of chronic hyperglycemia. Neuropathies associated with systemic illnesses—such as malignant disease‑related polyneuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث) and collagen vascular disease‑related polyneuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني)—also have their primary anatomic site in the peripheral nerves. Finally, severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة) is characterized by extensive peripheral nerve involvement, indicating a high‑grade disease process. Collectively, these entities illustrate that a wide spectrum of hereditary, metabolic, inflammatory, toxic, and systemic conditions converge on the peripheral nerves as a common site of pathology, emphasizing the importance of peripheral nerve assessment in diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2609
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerves (periferní nervy) serve as the primary anatomic site for a diverse group of neuropathic disorders, including Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ ماري‑ توث), cauda equina syndrome with neurogenic bladder (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية), drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), muscular atrophy and Bell’s palsy (وهن عضلي وبيل), diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), and malignant‑associated multifocal neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث). In addition, peripheral nerves are the specific finding site for several of these conditions: drug‑induced polyneuropathy, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, diabetic neuropathy, idiopathic progressive neuropathy, and malignant‑associated multifocal neuropathy all demonstrate clinical or diagnostic findings localized to the peripheral nerves. This pattern underscores that peripheral nerves are a common locus of both primary disease involvement and observable pathology across hereditary, metabolic, inflammatory, toxic, and neoplastic neuropathies, highlighting the importance of thorough peripheral nerve assessment in the diagnosis and management of patients with these disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1545
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of pancreatic and metabolic disorders. It includes “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) as the baseline form of the disease, as well as “مرض السكري نوع 2” (type 2 diabetes), which represents the most common adult‑onset variant characterized by insulin resistance. Severe acute metabolic decompensation can manifest as “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma), a life‑threatening state of extreme hyperglycemia and dehydration. Chronic complications of diabetes are reflected in “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy), a progressive nerve damage that can cause pain, sensory loss, and autonomic dysfunction. Hormonal dysregulation associated with pancreatic endocrine tumors is represented by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), often due to insulin‑secreting neoplasms, and “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma), a rare tumor that overproduces glucagon leading to characteristic skin and metabolic findings. Additionally, the list includes “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive facial hemi‑atrophy), a condition that, while not a classic diabetic complication, may be observed in the broader context of endocrine pancreatic disease. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations—from uncomplicated hyperglycemia to acute metabolic crises, chronic neuropathic damage, and endocrine neoplasms—associated with diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerves serve as the primary anatomic site for several distinct neurological disorders, including Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease and the condition referred to as وهن عضلي وبيل. In addition to these primary disease sites, peripheral nerves are the location of a wide spectrum of neuropathic findings. Metabolic and toxic insults are represented by alcoholic polyneuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي) and diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري). Autoimmune and inflammatory processes are exemplified by chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين). Malignant or paraneoplastic mechanisms can produce neuropathy in the context of cancer (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), and severe forms of nerve damage are described as severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة). Although an association was listed between peripheral nerves and acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي), this link is likely a misclassification, as bronchitis primarily involves the respiratory tract rather than peripheral nerve tissue. Collectively, these relationships underscore the central role of peripheral nerves in hereditary, metabolic, inflammatory, and neoplastic neuropathies, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation of nerve involvement in diverse clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1684
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerves (periferní nervy) serve as the primary anatomic site for several distinct neurological disorders, notably Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث) and cauda equina syndrome with neurogenic bladder (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية). In clinical practice, peripheral nerves are also the common location where a variety of neuropathic findings are identified. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين) typically presents with demyelination and inflammation of peripheral nerve fibers and roots. Diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري) reflects metabolic injury to peripheral nerves in patients with diabetes mellitus. Idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب) denotes a slowly advancing peripheral nerve degeneration of unknown cause, while severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة) describes a high‑severity form of peripheral nerve damage that may result from various etiologies. An association linking peripheral nerves to acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) appears inconsistent with established pathophysiology, as this respiratory condition primarily involves the bronchial mucosa rather than peripheral nerve tissue; therefore, this link is likely erroneous and not considered a true peripheral‑nerve finding. Collectively, these relationships underscore the central role of peripheral nerves in both hereditary and acquired neuropathies, highlighting the importance of accurate site identification for diagnosis, monitoring, and targeted therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide range of clinical findings, genetic factors, therapeutic considerations, and related disorders. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (disorder), and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, highlighting its relevance from birth onward. Genetic dysfunction of the human INSR protein is specifically associated with diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disease. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several severe complications of the lower extremities. Amputation of lower extremity includes child procedures such as toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), both of which are clinically associated with diabetes. Diabetic, idiopathic progressive, malignancy‑associated, collagen‑vascular disease, and severe polyneuropathies are all classified as peripheral neuropathies. “اعتلال مرتكز العظم” (bone‑centered neuropathy) is linked to tendon‑sheath inflammation, indicating an interaction between osseous neural changes and tenosynovial inflammation. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik is a drug‑induced polyneuropathy that manifests in the peripheral nerves (periferní nervy). The peripheral nerves are also the primary anatomic site for وهن عضلي وبيل (muscle weakness and related impairment). Several neuropathic disorders have their findings localized to the peripheral nerves, including اعتلال الأعصاب السكري (diabetic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني (multiple neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease), اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة (severe neuropathy), and التهاب العصب الأحادي (mononeuritis). Although التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي (acute infectious bronchitis) is listed with a finding site in the peripheral nerves, this condition primarily involves the respiratory tract and does not typically affect peripheral nerves; the association likely reflects a misclassification. Overall, the peripheral nerves serve as the common anatomical substrate for drug‑induced polyneuropathy, diabetic and collagen‑vascular neuropathies, severe and mononeuritic neuropathies, and muscle weakness, underscoring their central role in the clinical presentation and management of these neurologic and neuromuscular disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2238
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerves serve as the primary anatomic site for Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ ماري‑ توث) and are the common location of pathological findings in a variety of neuropathic disorders. Drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik) manifests with abnormalities in the peripheral nerves. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين) also produces lesions that are identified in the peripheral nerves, as do diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري) and severe peripheral neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة). Mononeuritis multiplex (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد) similarly involves the peripheral nerves. Although acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) primarily affects the respiratory tract, its inclusion among conditions linked to peripheral‑nerve findings likely reflects a misclassification rather than a true neuropathic involvement. Recognizing that peripheral nerves are the central site of disease activity for most of these entities is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted investigations, and appropriate therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2508
retrieved_docs.content: "Other disorders of nervous system" is a diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of peripheral nerve and neuromuscular conditions. Within this category are diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), a metabolic nerve damage caused by chronic hyperglycemia; multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), which reflects nerve involvement secondary to cancer; chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with root involvement (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), an immune‑mediated disorder characterized by demyelination of peripheral nerves; mononeuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي) and its multiple form (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد), inflammatory processes that affect a single nerve or several individual nerves; lower‑limb reflex atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), a pattern of muscle wasting and diminished reflexes in the legs; and Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث), a hereditary motor‑sensory neuropathy. An additional entry labeled "تكزز" is also classified under this group, though its specific meaning is unclear. Collectively, these entities illustrate that "Other disorders of nervous system" includes metabolic, neoplastic, inflammatory, hereditary, and idiopathic neuropathies, highlighting the importance of recognizing varied etiologies when evaluating peripheral nervous system pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2677
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is clinically associated with several serious complications and related conditions. Toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) frequently occurs in patients with diabetes, reflecting the disease’s impact on peripheral vascular health and wound healing. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is another common clinical manifestation, indicating the risk of diabetic retinopathy and vision loss. In addition, pancreatic disorders that are not themselves diabetes (pancreatic disorders (not diabetes)) are linked to diabetes, underscoring the broader involvement of pancreatic pathology in the disease’s metabolic dysregulation. Moreover, malfunction of the human insulin receptor protein (INSR protein, human) is associated with diabetes, highlighting a genetic and molecular contributor to the condition. Together, these relationships illustrate how diabetes encompasses vascular, ocular, pancreatic, and genetic dimensions that are critical for comprehensive patient assessment and management.
